reasoning:
The reference historical baseline for ACLED 30-day average fatalities in Nigeria is approximately 750, meaning the question requires more than ~7,550 fatalities in a single 30-day period. This is an extraordinarily high threshold. For context, all of Nigeria experiences roughly 10,000 political violence fatalities over an entire year according to ACLED data. Reaching ~7,550 fatalities in a single month would require a violence intensity far exceeding even the peak of the Boko Haram conflict in 2014-2015 (which maxed out around 3,000-4,000 in its deadliest months). 

Given that today is May 11, 2025, the first resolution window (ending May 18) is already nearly complete with no reports of massive casualty events anywhere close to this magnitude. The possibility of such a devastating spike occurring in the subsequent few months safely stays remarkably low. Without an ongoing uncontained mass civil war or genocide, achieving a 10x monthly spike is virtually impossible. Giving the minimum system-allowed probabilities of 0.05 for the nearest date, slightly stepping up to 0.08 for November out of sheer systemic/tail-end uncertainty.
